claims.claim: Deleting a page requires the Read and Delete page permissions
claims.location: Info callout before introductory steps, line 19
claims.evidence: WebPageAclPermissionEvaluator.cs line 118-123: DELETE is not in IndividuallyCheckedPermissions (WebsiteConstants.cs line 20-24 lists only READ and DISPLAY), so EvaluatePagePermission falls through to line 123 which checks grantedPermissions.Contains(WebPageAclPermissions.READ) && grantedPermissions.Contains(permission), i.e., READ + DELETE. WebPagesApplication.cs line 528 passes WebPageAclPermissions.DELETE to CheckAclPermission.

claims.claim: The Delete page permission is required for child pages that have their own permission settings (broken ACL inheritance)
claims.location: Section: Deleting a page with child pages, paragraph 2 (line 71)
claims.evidence: WebPageAclPermissionEvaluator.cs line 91-93: when evaluateChildPages=true, calls CheckAclSubtreePermission. Line 130 remark: 'For performance purposes, only web pages with broken inheritance are checked.' Line 146: InnerJoin<WebPageAclInfo> selects only child pages with their own ACL records (broken inheritance). Line 151: checks for DELETE permission specifically. WebPagesApplication.cs line 551 passes evaluateChildPermissions=true for DeletePageWithChildren.

claims.claim: If a child page does not inherit permissions from its parent, you must have the Delete permission set directly on that page
claims.location: Section: Deleting a page with child pages, paragraph 2, sentence 2 (line 71)
claims.evidence: Same source as above. The InnerJoin<WebPageAclInfo> in CheckAclSubtreePermission (line 146) means only pages with their own ACL record are checked — 'does not inherit permissions from its parent' accurately represents a page having its own ACL record. The HAVING clause (line 151) checks for the specific permissionName (DELETE), so 'Delete permission set directly on that page' is accurate.

claims.location: Info callout, line 19: {% page_link permissions_pagelevel_xp linkText="page permissions" %}

claims.claim: Cross-reference to permissions_pagelevel_xp identifier (child pages section)
claims.location: Section: Deleting a page with child pages, line 71: {% page_link permissions_pagelevel_xp linkText="page permission" %}

REC-005.notes: REC-005 recommends descriptive cross-reference link text. The link texts 'page permissions' and 'page permission' are descriptive and tell the reader what they will find. REC-005 also advises avoiding duplicate links to the same destination — the content has two links to permissions_pagelevel_xp in different sections (intro callout and child pages section), but these serve distinct contextual purposes for different aspects of permission requirements. Acceptable for a business audience who may navigate directly to either section.

domainInsightCheck.notes: No domain insights in knowledge-brief.json directly address delete page permissions or ACL inheritance topics.

notes: All technical claims trace cleanly to source code. The permission logic in WebPageAclPermissionEvaluator.cs confirms: (1) READ + DELETE is required for the page itself (line 123), (2) child pages with broken ACL inheritance (own WebPageAclInfo record) require DELETE permission specifically (CheckAclSubtreePermission, lines 131-157). The business-friendly phrasing 'their own permission settings' is an accurate representation of 'broken ACL inheritance' for the target business persona. Both cross-reference identifiers resolve correctly.
